description: In June, Charles V of the Habsburg dynasty is elected King of the Holy Roman Empire; by the time of his coronation in 1520 at the latest, he refers to himself as the
King Gustav I Vasa of Sweden breaks off contact with Rome. His country becomes Protestant in 1527.
The Peasants' Wars, which had already broken out in 1524, reach their peak. In southern and central Germany, and later also in Tyrol and Saxony, the uprisings are brutally suppressed.
The Imperial Diet in Speyer in August grants princes and cities leeway in religious matters until a national council is convened.

At the Imperial Diet of Västerås (Sweden), the church in Sweden and Finland is placed directly under the crown, and church property is transferred to the monarch. In July, the first Protestant university is inaugurated in Marburg under Landgrave Philip I.
In April, reformist princes protest at the Second Diet of Speyer against the maintenance of the Edict of Worms. Their protest gives rise to the term for the subsequent
In June, Protestant estates present the

("Confessio Augustana") — initiated by Philipp Melanchthon — to the Diet of Augsburg, which Luther, as an outlaw, is barred from attending. Hopes for its recognition are quickly dashed. The Catholic response comes even while the Diet is still in session. Under threat of force, the Emperor and the Catholic estates demand a return to Roman doctrine.
In February, Protestant estates form the
a defensive alliance against the Emperor.
Charles V, who sees the Empire threatened primarily by the Ottomans, relents. The "Nuremberg Truce" is thus a temporary religious truce.
Henry VIII of England marries Anne Boleyn against the Pope
The Danish king subjects his country to a unified church order drafted by the reformer Bugenhagen.
In June, Catholic imperial estates unite against the Schmalkaldic League.
Upon his return to Geneva, John Calvin introduces a new church order.
In December, Pope Paul III convenes the Council of Trent. In the wake of the Reformation, the Roman Catholic Church deliberates on its own reforms until 1563.
During a lull in foreign policy in July, the Emperor prepares for war against the Schmalkaldic League; the "Schmalkaldic War" begins.
In April, Charles V defeats the Protestant troops at the Battle of Mühlberg on the Elbe.
In March, Protestant estates ally with France against the Emperor in the so-called "Princes
and capture numerous cities. Charles V is forced to flee.
In August, Charles
In September, the
recognizes Lutheranism. Henceforth, the sovereigns decide the denomination of their subjects (cuius regio, eius religio).

Lutheran theologians and jurists are expelled from their congregations in the south of the Holy Roman Empire, just as the Huguenots were expelled from France starting in 1562 in the context of the religious wars there.
The so-called Huguenot Wars take place in France.
The Dutch struggle for freedom culminates in the
War'.
The Sejm (Polish Parliament) recognizes the equal coexistence of Protestant, Roman Catholic, and Orthodox faiths.
